title: Roast pheasant
subtitle: Barded with bacon, 35 minutes high heat
excerpt: Pheasant is a lean bird that dries quickly. Laying streaky bacon across the breast protects the meat while the fat bastes it from above. Ready in 35 minutes.
sourceNotes:
Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. Roast pheasant with lardons.

Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845). Roasting of game birds.

glossaryTerms.definition: Laying strips of fatty bacon over a lean bird or roast before cooking. The fat melts and bastes the meat from above, keeping it moist throughout the roasting time.
Leaving cooked meat to stand before carving, allowing the juices to redistribute evenly through the muscle fibres rather than running onto the board.

content.text: Pheasant is lean and benefits from careful treatment. The breast dries out faster than chicken, so the bacon barding is not decorative: it is the technique that makes the recipe work. A hen pheasant is plumper than a cock and usually better for roasting.

items.prepNote: for the base of the tin

content.text: Heat the oven to 200 degrees C fan. Remove the pheasant from the fridge 30 minutes before cooking. Pat dry with kitchen paper. Rub the softened butter all over the bird and season generously.
Tuck the thyme and bay leaves inside the cavity.
the breast by draping the bacon rashers in overlapping layers across the top of the bird, covering the breast completely.

Place the pheasant breast-side up in a roasting tin. Pour the stock around the base. Roast for 30 minutes, then remove the bacon to the side of the tin and return to the oven for a further 5 minutes to colour the breast.
The pheasant is done when the juices run clear at the thigh.
for 10 minutes before carving. Serve the crisp bacon alongside.

items.symptom: The breast is dry and stringy despite the bacon
items.cause: Overcooked. Pheasant is done at lower temperatures than chicken.
items.fix: 35 minutes total is enough for a 1 kg bird. Use a thermometer: 72 degrees C in the thigh is the target. Pull it out then.

items.symptom: The bacon has stuck to the breast and torn the skin
items.cause: Bacon was too thin or cooked on without enough butter underneath.
items.fix: Butter the breast first, then lay the bacon loosely. It should lift away cleanly after 30 minutes of cooking.

items.symptom: The pan juices have reduced to nothing during cooking
items.cause: Not enough stock in the base, or the tin was too small.
items.fix: Always add stock to the base. A generous 150 ml will give enough pan juices for a simple sauce after straining.

items.cause: Wild game bird with embedded lead shot from shooting.
items.fix: This is normal with wild pheasant. Warn guests to check their meat carefully before eating. Farmed pheasants are usually shot-free.

content.text: Stuff the cavity with a halved apple and a few juniper berries for an autumnal flavour. A cream sauce made from the pan juices with a splash of calvados makes an elegant alternative to gravy. Two pheasants feeds four people; scale the roasting time by just 5 minutes for two birds.

Pheasant season runs from October to February in the UK. It is a staple of country-house cooking, described by Victorian cookery writer Mrs Beeton as requiring careful attention to avoid dryness. The bird fell out of fashion in urban British cooking in the mid-twentieth century. It has only partially recovered, mainly through farmers market game sales and restaurant game menus.
